title: Solus Christus: The All-Sufficient Savior
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Solus Christus - Pastor Tito Lyro - Acts 5_4-12.txt

outline.point: I. The Human Tendency to Add to Christ
subpoints: A. The church perennially struggles with the exclusivity and all-sufficiency of Jesus Christ
B. The Roman Catholic sacramental system: seven ritual additions to Christ's completed work
C. First-century Judaism similarly obscured faith in God with performance requirements
D. Christ plus nothing equals everything; Christ plus something equals nothing

outline.point: II. Peter's Defense: Christ Is the Cornerstone (Acts 4:8-11)
subpoints: A. The healing of the lame man was good — done in the name and power of Jesus
B. You rejected him by crucifying him; God vindicated him by raising him from the dead
C. The empty tomb is the greatest apologetic: neither side could produce the body
D. Psalm 118:22-24 — the rejected stone became the chief cornerstone; this is the day the Lord has made
A. 'Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved'
B. All other religions lead to the same place — but that place is not salvation in God
C. John Newton's dying words: 'I am a great sinner and Christ is a great Savior'
D. The most important question: who do you say that Christ is? (Matthew 22:42)

outline.point: IV. Six Titles Describing Christ's All-Sufficiency
subpoints: A. The all-sufficient Shepherd who delivers and leads his sheep — even through the valley of darkness
B. The all-sufficient Husband who bears full responsibility for our debts and gives us all his riches
C. The all-sufficient Prophet who speaks and discloses the invisible God — no one knows God apart from him
D. The all-sufficient Priest who is both the intercessor and the offering — interceding on the basis of his own blood
E. The all-sufficient King who conquered through the cross and rules over all history for his kingdom
F. The all-sufficient Friend who finds the wandering sinner and gives eternal joy and comfort

summary:
Pausing from the 1 John series to consider a fruit of the 16th-century Reformation, this sermon takes Acts 4:1-12 as its text for the doctrine of Solus Christus — Christ alone saves. Pastor Lyro traces the church's perennial tendency to add things to Christ — a tendency visible in both first-century Judaism and the medieval sacramental system — and argues that the Reformers were simply restating apostolic truth when they insisted on Christ alone. The sermon's first half follows Peter's speech before the Sanhedrin: what we did was good; we did it in Jesus' name; you rejected him but God vindicated him by raising him from the dead; and therefore there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved.

The second half of the sermon develops a rich Christological portrait through six titles describing Christ's all-sufficiency. As the all-sufficient Shepherd, Christ leads his sheep through even the darkest valleys without abandoning them. As the all-sufficient Husband, he bears our debts and gives us his riches in a marriage where we bring only our sins. As the all-sufficient Prophet, he discloses the invisible God — without him, God cannot be known. As the all-sufficient Priest, he is both the intercessor and the sacrifice — the only mediator between God and man. As the all-sufficient King, he conquered through the cross and now rules all history for his glory. As the all-sufficient Friend, he finds the sinner wandering toward eternal death and gives him eternal joy. The sermon closes with the call to cling to Christ alone, shedding every addition — because to live is Christ.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Acts 4:1-12

If you're able, remains standing for the reading of God's Word. We're going to read Acts chapter 4, as we take a break from our 1 John series to consider a fruit of the 16th century Reformation. So here is the word of God, Acts chapter 4, starting at verse 1.

Now as they spoke to the people, the priests, the captain of the temple, and the Sadducees came upon them, being greatly disturbed that they taught the people and preached in Jesus the resurrection from the dead, and they laid hands on them and put them in custody until the next day, for it was already evening. However, many of those who heard the word believed, and the number of the men came to be about five thousand. And it came to pass on the next day, that their rulers, elders, and scribes, as well as Annas the high priest, Caiaphas, John, and Alexander, and as many as were of the family of the high priest, were gathered together at Jerusalem. And when they had set them in the midst, they asked, "By what power or by what name have you done this?" Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, said to them, "Rulers of the people and elders of Israel: if we this day are judged for a good deed done to a helpless man, by what means he has been made well, let it be known to you, and to all the people of Israel, that by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead, by him this man stands here before you whole. This is the stone which was rejected by your builders, which has become the chief cornerstone. Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men, by which we must be saved."

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father in heaven, thank you for your word. We pray that you would give us ears to hear what it says. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Courage to Seek Christ Alone

We are Protestants. We are not Roman Catholics. We are not Orthodox Christians. We are Protestants for a particular reason, and we draw our heritage back to the 16th century and through them to the apostolic church. Having the courage to be a Protestant requires the courage to seek Christ as the all-sufficient Savior. That's our goal for today.

This is a concept the Church of Jesus Christ struggles with perennially. We tend to be distracted from Christ. We tend to find new things that attract us instead of relying in the all-sufficient Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ.

By the time the 1500s arrived, the church had developed a complex system of sacraments — a complex system of rituals designed to complement Christ. In no period of church history did the church ever officially deny that faith in Christ was necessary for salvation. But she did deny that Jesus was the *also-sufficient* Savior. Most people are okay with Christ as long as they can add something to him. Through the centuries, the church developed seven acts that, apart from at least five of them, a Christian could not obtain salvation:

**Baptism** was said to forgive original sin and all sins to that point. Many tried to delay their baptism till their dying days. **Confirmation** allowed you to participate in the Lord's Supper. **The Eucharist** — necessary to partake of often; the current Roman Catholic minimum is once a year. **Penance** — auricular confession to a priest, followed by prescribed acts to pay for temporary forgiveness. Indulgences were penance that you could buy — Luther's great provocation. **Extreme unction** — anointing at death; too many people were surviving it, so the name was changed to "anointing of the sick." **Holy orders** — ordination. **Matrimony** — the seventh sacrament.

These were developed in addition to faith in Christ, but with time they virtually replaced faith in Christ in practice. When the Reformers returned to the scriptures — the doctrine of *Sola Scriptura* — they realized that the sacramental system was a devilish idea, and that salvation is in Christ alone. That's the doctrine that became known as *Solus Christus*.

The math: Christ plus nothing equals everything in your salvation. Christ plus something equals nothing in your salvation. Christ plus nothing equals heaven. Christ plus something equals hell.

But the reformers were not saying anything new. They were just stating what the apostles had stated in the New Testament. And that's what we see in Acts 4.

## I. The Human Tendency to Add to Christ

Peter and John had healed a lame man in the temple. That led to Peter preaching the second recorded sermon in Acts, thousands getting saved, and the religious leaders arresting Peter and John.

Before we continue, it is important to note that first-century Judaism had also added all kinds of performance requirements to faith in God — to the point that faith in God was completely obscured. The apostles found themselves in essentially the same place as the Reformers in the 1500s. Why this repetition in history? Because this is a tendency of the human heart. We tend to add things to what God wants us to believe.

## II. Peter's Defense: Christ Is the Cornerstone (Acts 4:8-11)

Peter's defense is actually an attack on the core of what the religious leaders believed. He says what they did was *good* — verses 8-9. Not bad, as the leaders claimed. And he says what they did, they did in the name of Jesus — by his authority and power — verses 10-11.

"Let it be known to you, and to all the people of Israel, that by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead — by him this man stands here before you whole."

You rejected him by crucifying him. God approved of him by raising him from the dead. That's the argument. The proof of Christ's all-sufficiency is the resurrection.

Want a proof for the existence of God, for the truth of the gospel? The empty tomb. Nobody can explain it. Everybody — secular or not — agrees the tomb was empty. If the enemies of Christ had removed the body, they could have said, "Here it is." If the apostles had taken the body, they would not have died for what they knew was a lie. Neither side produced the body because there was no body to produce. The best apologetic for the gospel is the empty tomb.

Peter confirms what he's saying by quoting Psalm 118:22-24: "The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone. This was the Lord's doing; it is marvelous in our eyes. This is the day the Lord has made; we will rejoice and be glad in it." That day — the day of the resurrection — is reflected every Lord's Day. It is the Lord's Day, the day on which we rejoice and are glad.

## III. No Other Name (Acts 4:12)

But Peter continues. Jesus is not just the stone on which things are built — he is everything. Verse 12: "Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men, by which we must be saved."

Notice how Peter repeats himself by referring to the name of Jesus. The name represents all that Jesus is and all that Jesus has done. But it also specifies: this name only. This person only. This work only.

All other religions lead to the same place. But that place is not salvation in God — because that only happens through Jesus Christ. So if you are trusting in anything else besides Jesus and Jesus alone, you are trusting in a failed enterprise that will only lead to judgment.

A useful test: if there were a proverbial pearly gate and Peter were standing in front of it, asking why he should let you in — if the first thing that comes to mind is anything other than Christ, you need to reconsider what you believe.

John Newton's last recorded words, written by his young friend William Jay: "My memory is nearly gone, but I remember two things: that I am a great sinner, and that Christ is a great Savior."

The greatest decision any human ever makes concerns the nature of Christ. Jesus asked the Pharisees in Matthew 22:42: "What do you think about the Christ? Whose Son is he?" The same question is put before every one of us. Everything hinges on that decision — with eternity in the balance.

## IV. Six Titles Describing Christ's All-Sufficiency

The all-sufficiency of Christ is demonstrated in his relationship to the Christian through at least six titles the Bible gives him.

**Christ is the all-sufficient Shepherd who delivers his sheep.** John 10 calls him the good shepherd. Hebrews 13:20-21 calls him the great shepherd of the sheep. Psalm 23 cannot be read without thinking of Christ. A sinner bound in slavery to his own sin is delivered by the shed blood of Christ and freed to walk, sustained by God, along the path to eternal rest. Even through the valley of darkness — not just the shadow of death, as some translations have it, but the valley of *darkness* — our all-sufficient shepherd is leading us.

**Christ is the all-sufficient Husband who will only wed himself to us.** He has taken full responsibility for our debts. His honor and riches and glory are now ours. To this marriage we bring our sins — that's the only thing we bring. Christ brings all the riches of the universe for now and forever. He deals with us with great affection, great love, tenderness, and sympathy.

**Christ is the all-sufficient Prophet who speaks to us.** We suppress the truth; we become futile and foolish; we worship creation instead of the Creator. And this is the state in which Christ found humanity. As the all-sufficient Prophet, he spoke and disclosed the invisible God. There is no way to know God apart from the Lord Jesus Christ. To know Christ is to know God.

**Christ is the all-sufficient Priest who died a criminal's death — your death, if you're a believer.** He is both the intercessor and the offering. He intercedes based on his own blood, coming before a holy God on our behalf on the basis of his sacrifice. Hebrews 10 shows us this. Because of this, there is one and only one ultimate and final mediator between God and man — Jesus Christ, the God-man.

**Christ is the all-sufficient King who reigns over the world.** He fought, he bled, he died — but in dying he conquered, destroying death. Through the cross, the King won. As Christ hung crucified, Satan thought he was winning. And yet it was through the cross that Christ attained victory. He is the King over the whole universe. Kings and politicians may be ignorant of his reign, but their ignorance makes his dominion no less real. Christ is the king in charge of all political elections and processes. The collapse of kingdoms and the commotion of revolutions all unfold according to a wisely determined plan whose final cause is the kingdom of God and Christ.

**Christ is the all-sufficient Friend who protects us.** He understands and seeks out sinners who are poor and puny. No weakness in his friends withholds Christ's free and endless love. Nothing in you — if you are a true believer — causes him to love you any less or any differently. As he said in the Upper Room Discourse: "No greater love has this, than to lay your life for your friends." Christ found us wandering in a godless desert, tripping toward eternal death. And he stepped in not only to save us but to give us eternal joy and comfort.

## Closing

To speak of the all-sufficiency of Christ is inevitably to see weaknesses in the present age and to foresee hopes in the future. If we are in love with Christ, if we see him as the all-sufficient Savior, we will see all kinds of hopelessness in this age — because this is the age of hopelessness and weakness. But also, if we are firmly embracing and clinging to the all-sufficiency of Christ, this faith stimulates our lives with the anticipation of the presence and the glory of Christ.

If Christ is our all-sufficient Savior, we will be able to pray with John: "Come quickly, Lord Jesus!" And the things of this life will, as the hymn says, "go strangely dim in the light of his glory and grace."

So we keep on looking to Christ and him alone, shearing away anything we might be tempted to add to him — because after all, to live is Christ. Not any Christ — to live is the all-sufficient Christ revealed in your life.

Let us pray together. Father, thank you for your word. Thank you for the Christ that your word reveals to us. May we be able to keep our eyes upon him. We pray that indeed to live will be Christ — the all-sufficient Christ revealed in your word. We pray that we would shed any idea that we can add anything to him, but come completely naked before you, knowing that he clothes us with all righteousness. We simply cling to the cross of your Son. We cling to Christ, to what he has done for us. We believe — and we pray: help us in our unbelief in those times when we attempt to contribute to our own salvation. We know there is nothing we can do, and yet that is how we come to you now. Open our eyes to see Christ even more clearly as we live for him. In whose name we pray, amen.
